title: Kth Largest Element in a Stream
description: Design a class to find the kth largest element in a stream. It is guaranteed that there will be at least k elements in the stream when the search for the kth largest element is called. Implement the KthLargest class: KthLargest(int k, int[] nums) initializes the object with the integer k and the stream of integers nums. int add(int val) appends the integer val to the stream and returns the element representing the kth largest element in the stream.

examples.input: KthLargest(3, [4,5,8,2]), add(3), add(5), add(10), add(9), add(4)

hints: Maintain a min-heap of size k that stores the k largest elements seen so far.
On initialization, push all elements into the heap and remove extras until size is k.
On add, if the new value is larger than the heap's root, replace it.
The heap's root is always the kth largest element.
timeComplexity: O(log k) for add, O(n log k) for initialization

title: Last Stone Weight
description: You are given an array of integers stones where stones[i] is the weight of the ith stone. We are playing a game with the stones. On each turn, we choose the heaviest two stones and smash them together. If the heaviest two stones have weights x and y with x <= y, then the result is: if x == y, both stones are destroyed; if x != y, the stone of weight x is destroyed, and the stone of weight y has new weight y - x. At the end of the game, there is at most one stone left. Return the weight of the last remaining stone. If there are no stones left, return 0.
hints: Use a max-heap to always get the two heaviest stones.
Pop the two largest, compute their difference, and push the result back if non-zero.
Continue until at most one stone remains.

title: Kth Largest Element in an Array
description: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the kth largest element in the array. Note that it is the kth largest element in the sorted order, not the kth distinct element. You must solve it in O(n) time complexity.
hints: Use a min-heap of size k to keep track of the k largest elements seen so far.
Initialize the heap with the first k elements.
For each remaining element, if it is larger than the heap's root, replace the root and heapify.
The root of the final heap is the kth largest element.

title: Meeting Rooms II
description: Given an array of meeting time intervals intervals where intervals[i] = [starti, endi], return the minimum number of conference rooms required.
hints: Sort the intervals by their start times.
Use a min-heap to track the end times of ongoing meetings.
For each meeting, if it starts after the earliest ending meeting, reuse that room (pop from heap).
Always push the current meeting's end time. The heap size at any point is the number of rooms in use.

title: Top K Frequent Elements
description: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the k most frequent elements. You may return the answer in any order.
hints: Count the frequency of each element using a hash map.
Use a min-heap of size k where each entry stores (frequency, element).
For each element, if the heap has fewer than k elements, push it. Otherwise, if its frequency is greater than the min-heap root, replace the root.
Extract all elements from the heap to get the result.

title: Kth Smallest Element in a Sorted Matrix
description: Given an n x n matrix where each of the rows and columns is sorted in ascending order, return the kth smallest element in the matrix. Note that it is the kth smallest element in the sorted order, not the kth distinct element.
hints: Start from the top-left corner (smallest element) and add it to a min-heap.
Track visited cells to avoid duplicates.
For each popped cell, add its right and bottom neighbors to the heap if not visited.
Pop k times to get the kth smallest element.

title: Task Scheduler
description: You are given a char array representing tasks CPU needs to do. It contains capital letters A-Z representing different tasks. Each task could be done in one interval. For each interval, the CPU could finish one task or be idle. However, there is a non-negative integer n that represents the cooldown period between two same tasks. Return the least number of intervals the CPU will take to finish all the given tasks.
hints: Count the frequency of each task.
The key observation is that the answer is determined by the most frequent task(s).
Calculate: (maxFreq - 1) * (n + 1) + count of tasks with max frequency.
The result is the maximum of this calculation and the total number of tasks.
timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the number of tasks
spaceComplexity: O(1) since there are at most 26 tasks

title: K Closest Points to Origin
description: Given an array of points where points[i] = [xi, yi] represents a point on the X-Y plane and an integer k, return the k closest points to the origin (0, 0). The distance between two points is the Euclidean distance. You may return the answer in any order.
hints: Calculate the squared distance for each point (avoids floating-point sqrt).
Use a max-heap of size k to store the k closest points seen so far.
For each point, if the heap has fewer than k elements, push it. Otherwise, if its distance is less than the max-heap root, replace the root.
The heap contains the k closest points at the end.

title: Merge k Sorted Lists
description: You are given an array of k linked-lists lists, each linked-list is sorted in ascending order. Merge all the linked-lists into one sorted linked-list and return it.
hints: Use a min-heap to always pick the smallest node among all current heads.
Push the head of each non-null list into the heap.
Pop the smallest, append it to the result, and push its next node if it exists.
Continue until the heap is empty.
timeComplexity: O(N log k) where N is total number of nodes

title: Find Median from Data Stream
description: The median is the middle value in an ordered integer list. If the size of the list is even, there is no middle value, and the median is the mean of the two middle values. Implement the MedianFinder class: void addNum(int num) adds the integer num from the data stream, double findMedian() returns the median of all elements so far.

examples.input: MedianFinder(), addNum(1), addNum(2), findMedian(), addNum(3), findMedian()

hints: Use two heaps: a max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half.
The max-heap stores the smaller half of the numbers, the min-heap stores the larger half.
Keep the heaps balanced: their sizes differ by at most 1.
If heaps are equal size, the median is the average of both tops. Otherwise, the larger heap's top is the median.
timeComplexity: O(log n) for addNum, O(1) for findMedian
